aliases: Lamb Kebab with Baked Cheese and Mashed Potato

english: Lamb Kebab with Baked Cheese and Mashed Potato

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic kebab food product class; Sultan Kebab is a specific restaurant preparation with lamb, baked cheese, and mashed potato not individually modelled in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (lamb meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor; the composite dish with cheese and mashed potato is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Sultan Kebab as a composite dish; not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Sultan Kebab is approximately 300 g, comprising a portion of spiced lamb, a layer of baked cheese, and a side of mashed potato.

nutrient_source.title: Sultan Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sultan Kebab (cr_id G00010365). Saturated fat not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground lamb (USDA SR Legacy #17066) as the closest approved-source analogue, yielding approximately 11.5 g/100 g saturated fat. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sultan Kebab as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contribution from mashed potato (GI ~78 for plain mashed potato per University of Sydney GI Database) blended with the very low carbohydrate contribution of the lamb and cheese components, which dilute the overall glycaemic response. The composite dish carbohydrate is 5.24 g/100 g, making the overall GI effectively low. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 15.72 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.1 g, classified as low GL. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Sultan Kebab; a single 300 g serving delivers approximately 3240 mg sodium, which exceeds the entire recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals and will acutely elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low GI (~45) support glycaemic stability, but high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sultan Kebab has a low carbohydrate load and low glycaemic index, making it broadly compatible with a type 2 diabetes meal plan in moderate portions; however, the high saturated fat content (~11.5 g/100 g) and very high sodium (1080 mg/100 g) warrant caution, and patients should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150–200 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g), high sodium (1080 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Sultan Kebab as an occasional food only; the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and moderate dietary cholesterol raises LDL-C and blood pressure simultaneously, and a 300 g serving would contribute approximately 34.5 g saturated fat — nearly double the recommended daily limit of 20 g for CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid; high saturated fat and sodium amplify systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise Sultan Kebab consumption as the lamb base provides arachidonic acid that amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways, and the high sodium load (1080 mg/100 g) worsens joint tissue oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sultan Kebab is a Middle Eastern-inspired composite meat dish served in Singapore restaurants, consisting of mildly spiced minced or ground lamb that is grilled or baked, topped with melted baked cheese, and accompanied by creamy mashed potato. The dish is rich and savoury with a tender, juicy lamb base, a slightly crisp cheese crust, and a smooth, buttery potato component. Per 100 g, it delivers 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (of which approximately 11.5 g is saturated), 5.24 g carbohydrate, 2.85 g sugar, and a notably high sodium content of 1080 mg. The dietary fibre content is negligible at 0 g/100 g. The dish is high in calcium (118 mg/100 g) due to the cheese component and provides moderate iron (1.15 mg/100 g) from the lamb. Clinically, the very high sodium content makes it unsuitable for hypertensive patients without significant modification. The high saturated fat content raises cardiovascular concern. For type 2 diabetes, the low carbohydrate load and low glycaemic index (~45) are favourable, but the saturated fat burden requires portion control. The dish is halal-suitable and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner main course. It is listed on Singaporean Middle Eastern restaurant menus and is catalogued in the HPB Singapore Foodo database under food group Meat and Alternatives.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sultan Kebab?
Is Sultan Kebab suitable for diabetics?
Is Sultan Kebab high in sodium?
What is the fat content of Sultan Kebab?
Middle Eastern lamb kebab with cheese and mashed potato

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010365, confidence 0.95 per source record). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked ground lamb as the closest approved-source analogue and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate documented with full reasoning. All other fields are grounded in approved sources.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this composite Sultan Kebab dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010365) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (11.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked ground lamb (#17066) as the closest approved-source analogue; the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source record — the dish is dominated by lamb and cheese with minimal plant fibre. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sultan Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this dish and detailed micronutrient data for this specific composite preparation are not available in any approved source as of 2026-04-16. 6. This dish is classified as Middle Eastern cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided per Veritas Singaporean dish multilingual rules.
